لسی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀮𑀲𑁆𑀲𑀻 (*lassī), likely from 𑀮𑀲 (lasa, “stickiness”) (whence Hindi लस (las)), from Sanskrit लश (laśa, “gum, resin”)

لطف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لُطْف (lutf), from Arabic لُطْف (luṭf).

ex.u: لُطْفاً، اَپْنی دَوا وَقْت پَر لِیجِیے
ex.r: lutfan, apnī davā vaqt par lījiye
ex.e: Take your medicine on time, please

لطفا.e: Borrowed from Arabic لُطْفًا (luṭfan), adverbial accusative of لُطْف (luṭf).
Borrowed from Arabic لُطْفًا (luṭfan), adverbial accusative of لُطْف (luṭf).

لطوریا.e: From Ancient Greek λειτουργία (leitourgía), from λειτ- (leit-), from λαός (laós, “people”) + -ουργός (-ourgós), from ἔργον (érgon, “work”) (the public work of the people done on behalf of the people).

senses.g: kindness, grace (of God; related to لَطَف (lataf))

لطیفہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لطیفه (latīfa), from Arabic لَطِيفَة (laṭīfa).

لعب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لعب (la'b), from Arabic لَعْب (laʕb).

لغت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لُغَت (luġat), from Arabic لُغَة (luḡa). First attested in 1707 as Middle Hindi لغت (lġt /⁠luġat⁠/).

لغو.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لغو (laġw), from Arabic لَغْو (laḡw). Related to لغوی (luğavī, “belonging to a language”).
Borrowed from Classical Persian لغو (laġw), from Arabic لَغْو (laḡw). Related to لغوی (luğavī, “belonging to a language”).

senses.g: belonging to a language, literal, verbal, lexical

لغوی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لغوی (luġawī), from Arabic لُغَوِيّ (luḡawiyy). Related to لغو (lağv, “nonsense”).

لفافہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لِفَافَه (lifāfa), itself from Arabic لِفافَة (lifāfa).

ex.u: مَیں دو لَفْظوں میں یہ بَیاں نَہِیں کَر سَکْتا۔
ex.r: ma͠i do lafzõ mẽ ye bayā̃ nahī̃ kar saktā.
ex.e: I cannot express this in only two words.

لفظ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لَفْظ (lafz), from Arabic لَفْظ (lafẓ).

senses.g: a male given name, Luqman, from Arabic

لکڑی.e: Inherited from Apabhramsa लक्कुड (lakkuḍa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-), from Prakrit 𑀮𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀼𑀟 (lakkuḍa), from Sanskrit लकुट (lakuṭa). Compare Gujarati લાકડું (lākḍũ, “wood”), Marathi लाकूड (lākūḍ, “wood”).

senses.g: Lakdi ka pul (a neighbourhood of Hyderabad, Telangana, India)
Used other than figuratively or idiomatically: see لَکْڑی (lakṛī), کا (kā), پُل (pul).

لکڑی کا پل.e: Literally, “the wooden bridge”, referring to a railway bridge.

senses.g: Lakshadweep (an archipelago and union territory of India in the Arabian Sea)

لکشادیپ.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit लक्षद्वीप (lakṣadvīpa). Compare Kashmiri لَکشادیٖپ (lakśādīp) and Punjabi ਲਕਸ਼ਦੀਪ (lakśadīp) / لَکْشَدِیپ (lakśadīp).

senses.g: a female given name, Lakshmi, from Sanskrit, of Hindu usage
to write — to note down, write down

ex.u: یہ لِکھِئے
ex.e: (please) write it down

senses.g: to write — to enter (in an account book; log; computer etc.)
to compose (ie. a poem, poetry, letter etc.)

لکھنا.e: Urdu verb set لِکْھنا (likhnā) لِکھانا (likhānā) لِکْھوَانا (likhvānā) Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀮𑀺𑀳𑀤𑀺 (lihadi), from Sanskrit लिखति (likháti). Cognate with Nepali लेख्नु (lekhnu).

senses.g: Lucknow (the state capital of Uttar Pradesh, India)

لکھنؤ.e: Uncertain, perhaps either inherited from Sanskrit लक्ष्मणपुर (lakṣmaṇapura, “city of Lakshmana”), or perhaps from लक्ष्मणवती (lakṣmaṇavatī, “fortunate”).

senses.g: a person from or an inhabitant of Lucknow

ex.u: وہ تین دن لگاتار جگا رہا۔
ex.r: woh tīn din lagātār jagā rahā.
ex.e: He stayed awake for three days continuously.

ex.u: مکھن لگانا
ex.e: to apply butter

ex.u: اُسے کوئی کام پہ لگا ود
ex.r: use koī kām pih lagā do
ex.e: Put him to some work

ex.u: پروگرام لگانا
ex.e: to put on (the) program

ex.u: اُس نے ویڈیو یوٹیوب پر لگا دی
ex.r: us ne vīḍiyo yūṭiyūb par lagā dī
ex.e: He posted the video on YouTube.

ex.u: شرط لگانا
ex.e: to bet

ex.u: وقت لگانا
ex.e: to take time

لگانا.e: Urdu verb set لگنا لاگنا لگانا لَگوانا (lagvānā) Inherited from Sanskrit लागयति (lāgayati).

ex.u: مُسَلْمان، ہِنْدُوون کے ساتھ دُرْگاپُوجا میں شَرِیک ہوتے تہے اَور شادی بْیاہ وَغَیرَہ کے مَوقَع پَر بَرَہْمَنوں سے شُبھ لَگَن اَور سَعدِ ساعَت دَریافْت کِیا کَرتے تھے.
ex.e: Islamic Culture in the Indian Subcontinent

لگنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀮𑀕𑁆𑀕𑀤𑀺 (laggadi), from Sanskrit लग्यति (lagyati). Cognate with Nepali लाग्नु (lāgnu), Punjabi ਲੱਗਣਾ (laggaṇā), Gujarati લાગવું (lāgvũ), Marathi लागणे (lāgṇe), Assamese লগা (loga).

senses.g: inflection of لگنا (lagnā): — masculine plural perfective participle
inflection of لگنا (lagnā): — masculine plural perfect indicative
inflection of لگنا (lagnā): — second/third-person singular subjunctive
inflection of لگنا (lagnā): — third-person singular present/future imperative

لمبا.e: From the Prakrit लम्बओ and Sanskrit लम्ब (lamba) + -क (-ka).

senses.g: lustful, unchaste, addicted to sexual pleasure

لمحہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لَمْحَه (lamha), from Arabic لَمْحَة (lamḥa).

senses.g: London (the capital city of the United Kingdom; the capital city of England)

لندن.e: Borrowed from English London, possibly through Persian لندن (landan).

ex.u: میرا لَنْڈ کھَڑا ہو گَیا۔
ex.e: I got a boner.

لنڈ.e: Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀮𑀡𑁆𑀟 (*laṇḍa, “penis”). May be related to Sanskrit लङ्ग (laṅga, “union”) and लण्ड (laṇḍa, “excrement”).

لنگ.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀮𑀺𑀁𑀕 (liṃga), from Sanskrit लिङ्ग (liṅga).

senses.g: A cultivar of mango (Mangifera indica) known as Benarasi langra

لنگڑا.e: According to local folklore, named for a physically disabled لَن٘گْڑا (laṅgṛā, “crippled”) farmer, who first cultivated the variety.

لنگڑانا.e: Inherited from Sanskrit लङ्गति (laṅgati) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀟- (-ḍa-). Related to لَن٘گْڑا (laṅgṛā, “crippled, limp”).

لوبیا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لوبیا (lōbiyā), from Ancient Greek λόβια (lóbia).

senses.g: inflection of لُوٹْنَا (lūṭnā, “to loot”): — masculine singular perfective participle
inflection of لُوٹْنَا (lūṭnā, “to loot”): — masculine singular perfect indicative : looted
inflection of لَوٹنَا (lauṭnā, “to return; come back”): — masculine singular perfective participle
inflection of لَوٹنَا (lauṭnā, “to return; come back”): — masculine singular perfect indicative : returned; came back
lota, pot (used to hold water, especially for istinja)

ex.u: ایک لوٹَا پَانِی
ex.e: a bucketful of water

لوٹا.e: Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀮𑁄𑀝𑁆𑀝 (*loṭṭa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-). Cognate with Punjabi لوٹّا (loṭṭā) / ਲੋੱਟਾ (loṭṭā), Nepali लोहोटो (lohoṭo), Marathi लोटा (loṭā).

لوٹنا.e: Urdu verb set لُٹْنا (luṭnā) لُوٹْنا (lūṭnā) لُٹانا (luṭānā) Inherited from Prakrit 𑀮𑀼𑀝𑁆𑀝𑀇 (luṭṭaï).
Urdu verb set لَوٹْنا (lauṭnā) لَوٹانا (lauṭānā) Inherited from Old Hindi लौटना (lauṭanā).

senses.g: the tablet of the world; the world as seen as a tablet

ex.u:
یارب زمانہ مجھ کو مٹاتا ہے کس لیے
لوح جہاں پہ حرف مکرر نہیں ہوں میں
yā-rab zamānā mujh ko miṭātā hai kis lie
ex.r: lauh-e-jahā̃ pe harf-e-mukarrar nahī̃ hū̃ ma͠i
ex.e:
oh Lord, why does time erase me
on the tablet of the world I am not a (mistakenly) repeated letter

senses.g: Lodhran (a city in Lodhran district, Punjab, Pakistan)
Loralai (a city in Loralai district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

لوری.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀮𑁄𑀭 (lora), 𑀮𑁄𑀮 (lola) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-), from Sanskrit लोल (lola).

senses.g: Lusail (a city in Al Daayen, Qatar)
a male given name from Arabic

لوقا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لُوقَا (lūqā), from Arabic لُوقَا (lūqā), from Classical Syriac ܠܘܩܐ, from Ancient Greek Λουκᾶς (Loukâs).

senses.g: Lok Sabha (the lower house of the Parliament of India)

لوک سبھا.e: Sanskritic formation from لوک (lok, “the people; public”) + سَبھا (sabhā, “house”).

لوک شاہی.e: Coined after تانا شاہی (tānā śāhī), from لوک (lōk, “public, folk”) + شاہی (śāhī, “rule, reign, -cracy”).

senses.g: public honour; adherence to social obligation

ex.u: تیرا لوگ پردیس گیا ھے
ex.e: Your man has gone abroad

لوگ.e: Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit लोक (loka). Doublet of لوک (lok). First attested in c. 1503 as Middle Hindi لوگ (log).

ex.u: یکایک یہ کھلونا کپڑے یا لوہے یا ربڑ یا پلاسٹک کی بنی ہوئی شے نہیں رہ جاتا بلکہ باقاعدہ سانس لینے، ہنسنے، رونے، بگڑنے یا پیار کرنے لگتا ہے۔
ex.e: Suddenly, this toy is no longer an object [deduced to something merely] made from cloth or metal, or rubber, or plastic, but rather begins to breathe, laugh, cry, corrode or love.

ex.u: لوہا صاف ہوا کیا ہر ایک عازم دشت معاف ہوا
ex.e: [Indeed] the cleansing of the sword was as if every pilgrim of the plains was forgiven

ex.u: جن کی شجاعت کا لوہا تمام ملک میں مشہور تھا۔
ex.e: Whose pomp of courage was famous across the country.

senses.g: clothes iron (appliance used by tailors and washers)

لوہا.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi لوہا (lvha /⁠lohā⁠/) (c. 1564) / لوہ (lvh /⁠loh⁠/) (c. 1435), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀮𑁄𑀳 (loha), from Sanskrit लो॒ह (lohá).
Inherited from Middle Hindi لوہا (lvha /⁠lohā⁠/) (c. 1564) / لوہ (lvh /⁠loh⁠/) (c. 1435), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀮𑁄𑀳 (loha), from Sanskrit लो॒ह (lohá).

لوہار.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀮𑁄𑀳𑀸𑀭 (lohāra), from Sanskrit लोहकार (lohakāra).

لہجہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لَهْجَه (lahja), from Arabic لَهْجَة (lahja).

لہذا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لِهٰذا (lihāzā), from Arabic لِهٰذَا (lihāḏā).

لہو.e: First attested in c. 1500 as Middle Hindi لہو (luhū /⁠lhū⁠/) / لوہو (lohū), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀮𑁄𑀳𑀺𑀅 (lohia), ultimately from Sanskrit लोहित (lṓhita), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *Hráwdʰitas, from Proto-Indo-European *h₁rewdʰ- (“red”).

senses.g: Leh (one of the capitals and largest city in the union territory of Ladakh, India)
Liaqatabad (a neighbourhood of Karachi, Sindh, Pakistan)

لیاقت آباد.e: Named after First Prime Minister of Pakistan Liaquat Ali Khan, from لِیَاقَت (liyāqat) + ـآباد (-ābād).

لیبیا.e: Borrowed from either English Libya or Arabic لِيبِيَا (lībiyā).

senses.g: Leicester (a city in Leicestershire, East Midlands, England)

لیکن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لیکِن (lēkin), from Arabic لٰكِنْ (lākin).

لیکھنی.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit लेखनी (lekhanī), feminine of लेखन (lekhana).

senses.g: Lille (the departmental capital of Nord department, France; the regional capital of Hauts-de-France)

لیل.e: Borrowed from English Lille, ultimately taken from French Lille (with possible influence from Arabic لِيل (līl)).

senses.g: a female given name, Layla or Laila, equivalent to English Leila
a female given name, Layla or Laila, equivalent to English Leila — Layla al-Aamiriya (the heroine of the romance Layla and Majnun.)

لیلی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لَیلیٰ (laylā), from Arabic لَيْلَى (laylā). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi لیلیٰ (lyly /⁠lailā⁠/).
Borrowed from Classical Persian لَیلیٰ (laylā), from Arabic لَيْلَى (laylā). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi لیلیٰ (lyly /⁠lailā⁠/).

senses.g: Juliet (a woman who is with a great lover.)

لیلی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لَیلیٰ (laylā), from Arabic لَيْلَى (laylā). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi لیلیٰ (lyly /⁠lailā⁠/).

لیموں.e: Derived from Classical Persian لیمو (limu). Doublet of نیمبو (nīmbū) and لیمن (laiman).

senses.g: to pick up (a person or an item to take somewhere)

لینا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀮𑁂 (le) + ـنا (-nā, verbal suffix), the first element of which was adapted from 𑀮𑀳 (laha), from Sanskrit लभ् (labh, “to gain, to possess”), by analogy with 𑀤𑁂 (de, “giving”) and 𑀡𑁂 (ṇe, “taking”).

senses.g: Lyon (the capital city of Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes, France)

لیون.e: Borrowed from English Lyon, taken from French Lyon.

لیوں.e: Borrowed from French Lyon. Doublet of لیون (liyon).

senses.g: Layyah, a city in Punjab, Pakistan. It is the capital of Layyah District
Liège (a city, a province of Wallonia, Belgium)

ex.u: (learned borrowing)

لیئج.e: Borrowed from French Liège, likely influenced by English Liège.

senses.g: inflection of لینا (lenā): — masculine plural perfective participle
inflection of لینا (lenā): — masculine plural perfect indicative
inflection of لینا (lenā): — masculine plural perfective participle
inflection of لینا (lenā): — masculine plural perfect indicative
inflection of لینا (lenā): — second/third-person singular present subjunctive
inflection of لینا (lenā): — second-person singular present imperative
The thirty-first letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ل and followed by ن.

ماتا.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit माता (mātā), nominative singular of मातृ (mātṛ).

senses.g: a women's gathering for a good deed or bad occasion

ماتم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ماتَم (mātam), from Arabic مَأْتَم (maʔtam). Compare Punjabi ਮਾਤਮ (mātam) / ماتَم (mātam), Gujarati માતમ (mātam).

ماتھا.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi ماتھا (matha /⁠māthā⁠/) (c. 1503), from Prakrit 𑀫𑀢𑁆𑀣𑀬 (matthaya), from Sanskrit मस्तक (mastaka).

ماٹی.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀫𑀝𑁆𑀝𑀻 (maṭṭī), from Sanskrit मृत्ति॑का (mṛ́ttikā). Doublet of مِٹِّی (miṭṭī).

ex.u: اس اعتبار سے ہر آدمی کا ماحول اس کی دنیا 'اس کی کائنات ہوتا ہے' جس کے ساتھ اس کی محبت بے حد فطری اور قابل فہم بات ہے۔
ex.e: In this sense, every man's environment is his world, "is his universe" with which his love is exceptionally natural and understandable.

senses.g: circumstance, condition (surroundings in which one lives)

ex.u: زبان اور فن پر بھر پور دستری حاصل ہونے کی وجہ سے حیات نے اردو کے افسانوی ماحول کو بالکل سازگار بنایا۔
ex.e: Because of his profound knowledge in language and art, Hayat made Urdu's fictitious genre absolutely befitting.

ماحول.e: Borrowed from Arabic مَا حَوْلَ (mā ḥawla, literally “what is around”).

ماخولیا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَاخُولِیَا (māxūliyā), from Arabic مَاخُولِيَا (māḵūliyā), from Ancient Greek μελαγχολία (melankholía). Doublet of مَالِیخُولِیَا (mālīxūliyā).

مادر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مادر (mādar), from Old Persian 𐎶𐎠𐎫𐎠 (mātā). Cognate to Sanskrit मातृ (mā́tṛ), Hindi माता (mātā).

مادہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَادَه (māda). Doublet of مَاتَا (mātā).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مَادَّه (mādda), from Arabic مَادَّة (mādda).

مادیت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَادِّیَت (māddiyat), from Arabic مَادِّيَّة (māddiyya). By surface analysis, مَادَّہ (mādda) + ـِیَت (-iyat)

مارخور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مارخوار (mār-xʷār, literally “snake-eater”), variant of مارخور.

senses.g: guidance, direction; literally, showing the road

مارگ درشن.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit मार्गदर्शन (mārgadarśana). By surface analysis, مارْگ (mārg, “path, road”) + دَرْشَن (darśan, “watching”). Compare Gujarati માર્ગદર્શન (mārgadarśan).

مارنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀫𑀸𑀭𑁂𑀇 (mārei), from Sanskrit मारयति (mārayati), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *māráyati, from Proto-Indo-European *moréyeti, from *mer- + *éyeti.

مارونی.e: Borrowed from Arabic مَارُونِيّ (mārūniyy). By surface analysis, مارون (mārūn) + ـی (-ī).
Borrowed from Arabic مَارُونِيّ (mārūniyy). By surface analysis, مارون (mārūn) + ـی (-ī).

senses.g: Moscow (a federal city, the capital of Russia)

ماسی.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀫𑀸𑀉𑀲𑀺 (māusi), from Sanskrit मातुःष्वसृ (mātuḥṣvasṛ), from मातुः॑ (mā́tuḥ, “mother's”, gen.sg) + स्वसृ॑ (svásṛ, “sister”). Sense 3 developed through association with regional languages Punjabi ماسی (māsī) and Sindhi ماسِي (māsī), and through the relationship between language and class in Pakistan.

ماضی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ماضی (māzī), from Arabic مَاضِي (māḍī). Cognate with Gujarati માજી (mājī), Kannada ಮಾಜಿ (māji), Marathi माजी (mājī), Telugu మాజీ- (mājī-).
Borrowed from Classical Persian ماضی (māzī), from Arabic مَاضِي (māḍī). Cognate with Gujarati માજી (mājī), Kannada ಮಾಜಿ (māji), Marathi माजी (mājī), Telugu మాజీ- (mājī-).

مال.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مال (māl), from Arabic مَال (māl).

ex.u: ہر روز مالٹا کھانا چاہیے۔
ex.e: An orange should be eaten every day.

senses.g: the orange cultivar bloodred Malta, Maltaise Sanguine, Portugaise
clementine, Seville orange, sweet orange, tangerine

مالٹا.e: Borrowed from English, Italian, and Maltese Malta, from Arabic مَالِطَا (māliṭā), from Latin Melita, from Ancient Greek Μελίτη (Melítē).

senses.g: Malta (an archipelago and country in Southern Europe, in the Mediterranean Sea)
Malta (the largest island in the Maltese Archipelago)

مالٹا.e: Borrowed from English, Italian, and Maltese Malta, from Arabic مَالِطَا (māliṭā), from Latin Melita, from Ancient Greek Μελίτη (Melítē).

senses.g: Maldives (an island country in the Indian Ocean, South Asia; capital: Malé)

مالدیپ.e: Compare Bengali মালদ্বীপ (maldip) and Hindi मालदीव (māldīv).

senses.g: alternative form of مالدیپ (māldīp): Maldives (an archipelago and country in South Asia, located in the Indian Ocean off the coast of India)

مالک.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مالک (mālik), from Arabic مَالِك (mālik, “owner”).

مالیخولیا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مالیخولیا (mālīxūliyā), ultimately from Ancient Greek μελαγχολία (melankholía). Doublet of ماخُولِیَا (māxūliyā).

ماما.e: Inherited from Sanskrit माम (māma), from Proto-Dravidian *māma.
From ماں (mā̃), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑀸𑀤𑀸 (mādā), from Sanskrit मातृ (mātṛ, “mother”), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *máHtā, from Proto-Indo-European *méh₂tēr.

ماموں.e: From مامُو (māmū), an endearing diminutive of ماما (māmā), from Sanskrit माम (māma), itself Proto-Dravidian *māma.

ex.u: چناچه سنه ۱۷۷۰ عیسوئي کو مانچسٿر اور لیورپول شہر کے درمیان ایک نہر کھودنے کي
ex.r: cunācih sanh 1770 ʻīsva'ī ko māncisṭar aur līvarpūl śahar ke darmīyān ek nahar khodne kī
ex.e: Therefore in 1770 A.D. [a plan] to dig a canal between the cities of Manchester and Liverpool

مانچسٹر.e: Borrowed from English Manchester, influenced by languages such as Punjabi مانچَسْٹَر (māncasṭar) / مَنْچَسْٹَر (mancasṭar) or Pahari-Potwari مَنْچَسْٹَر (mancasṭar), as opposed to the direct borrowing of مینچسٹر (maincisṭar).

ماندہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَانْدَه (mānda), past participle of مَانْدَن (māndan, “to stay, remain”).

مانس.e: From Sanskrit मनुष्य (manuṣya), likely from a derivative of Proto-Indo-European *mon- (“man”).

senses.g: Mansehra (a city in Mansehra district, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan)
ask (hand in marriage of a girl)

مانگ.e: Deverbal from مانگنا (māṉgnā, “to request; ask; order”).

senses.g: to betroth; seek for the hand in marriage

مانگنا.e: Urdu verb set مان٘گْنا (māṅgnā) مَن٘گْوَانا (maṅgvānā) Etymology tree Proto-Indo-Iranian *mr̥gás Sanskrit मृग (mṛga)vṛd. Sanskrit मार्ग (mārga) Sanskrit मार्ग॑ति (mā́rgati) Prakrit 𑀫𑀕𑁆𑀕𑀇 (maggaï) Urdu مانگنا Inherited from Prakrit 𑀫𑀕𑁆𑀕𑀇 (maggaï), from Sanskrit मार्ग॑ति (mā́rgati), from मार्ग (mārga), a vṛddhi derivative of मृग (mṛga), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *mr̥gás. Cognate with Assamese মাগা (maga), Bengali মাঙ্গা (maṅga), Gujarati માંગવું (mā̃gvũ), Kachchi મંગણૂ (maṅgṇū), Kashmiri مَنٛگُن (mangun), Marathi मागणे (māgṇe), Nepali माग्नु (māgnu), Odia ମାଗିବା (māgibā), and Punjabi ਮੰਗਣਾ (maṅgṇā).

ماننا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑀡𑁆𑀡𑀤𑀺 (maṇṇadi), from Sanskrit मन्यते (mányate), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *mányatay, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *mányatay, from Proto-Indo-European *mn̥yo-, from *men- (“to think”).

ماں.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑀸𑀤𑀸 (mādā), from Sanskrit मातृ (mātṛ), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *máHtā, from Proto-Indo-European *méh₂tēr. Doublet of ماتا (mātā) and مادَر (mādar). Compare Punjabi ਮਾਂ (mā̃)/ماں (māṉ), Sindhi ماء (māʾu), Bengali মা (ma), Gujarati મા (mā).

ماہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ماه (māh, “moon”). Compare Baluchi ماہ (máh), Pashto مياشت (myāšt) and Punjabi ماہ (māh).

senses.g: having forehead as bright as the moon
an exquisitely beautiful person, handsome, pretty, gorgeous

ماہ‌جبیں.e: ماہ (māh, “moon”) + جبین (jabīn, “forehead”) Borrowed from Classical Persian ماهجبین (mâh-jabīn, literally “moon-like forehead”), ultimately compound of Classical Persian ماه (mâh, “moon”) and Arabic جبين (jabīn, “forehead”). Doublet of ماہسیما (māh-sīmā).

ماہر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ماهر (māhir), from Arabic مَاهِر (māhir).
Borrowed from Classical Persian ماهر (māhir), from Arabic مَاهِر (māhir).

ماہرین.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَاهِرِین (māhirīn), from Arabic مَاهِرِين (māhirīn).

ماء.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَاء (mā'), from Arabic مَاء (māʔ).

مایوس.e: Borrowed from Persian مایوس, from Arabic مأيوس.

senses.g: result, consequence, place of return, end

مآل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَآل (maāl), borrowed from Arabic مَآل (maʔāl).

مبارزہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُبارَزَه (mubāraza), from Arabic مُبَارَزَة (mubāraza)

senses.g: happy (on congratulations of anniversaries or occasions)

ex.u: سالگرہ مبارک
ex.e: Happy birthday

ex.u: سو مبارک ہو تم کو
ex.e: Hence, felicitations to you

مبارک.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مبارک (mubārik), from Arabic مُبَارَك (mubārak).

مبارکباد.e: From Classical Persian مبارک باد (mubārak bād).

مبلغ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُبَلِّغ (muballiġ, “missionary”), itself from Arabic مُبَلِّغ (muballiḡ).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مَبْلَغ (mablaġ), from Arabic مَبْلَغ (mablaḡ).

ex.u: یہاں مت بیٹھو!
ex.e: Don't sit here!

مت.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀫𑀁𑀢 (maṃta)), an extension of Ashokan Prakrit 𑀫𑀸 (mā), from Sanskrit मा (mā́), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *máH, from Proto-Indo-European *méh₁.
Borrowed from Sanskrit मत (mata), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *matás, from Proto-Indo-European *mn̥-tós.

متجسس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian متجسس (mutajassis), from Arabic مُتَجَسِّس (mutajassis).

متحدہ.e: From Classical Persian متحده (muttahida), ultimately from Arabic مُتَّحِدَة (muttaḥida)

senses.g: United Arab Emirates (a country in Asia)

متحدہ عرب امارات.e: Calque of Arabic الْإِمَارَات الْعَرَبِيَّة الْمُتَّحِدَة (al-ʔimārāt al-ʕarabiyya l-muttaḥida).

متر.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit मित्र (mitra). Compare Classical Persian مهر (mihr).

مترجم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُتَرْجِم (mutarjim), from Arabic مُتَرْجِم (mutarjim).

senses.g: known to each other, already introduced, mutually known, widely known

متعارف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian متعارف (muta'ārif), from Arabic مُتَعَارِف (mutaʕārif).

متعدد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُتَعَدِّد (muta'addid), borrowed from Arabic مُتَعَدِّد (mutaʕaddid), from تَعَدَّدَ (taʕaddada).

senses.g: concerning, belonging to, relating to, attached or otherwise connected to

متعلق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُتَعَلِّق (muta'alliq), from Arabic مُتَعَلِّق (mutaʕalliq).

متفرق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُتَفَرِّق (mutafarriq), borrowed from Arabic مُتَفَرِّق (mutafarriq), from تَفَرَّقَ (tafarraqa).

متنوع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُتَنَوِّع (mutanawwi'), from Arabic مُتَنَوِّع (mutanawwiʕ).

متوالا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀫𑀢𑁆𑀢𑀯𑀸𑀮 (mattavāla) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit *मत्तपाल (*mattapāla).
Inherited from Prakrit 𑀫𑀢𑁆𑀢𑀯𑀸𑀮 (mattavāla) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit *मत्तपाल (*mattapāla).

senses.g: following one after the other, successive, continued, consecutive, uninterrupted

متوالی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُتَوَالِی (mutawālī), borrowed from Arabic مُتَوَالٍ (mutawālin), from تَوَالَى (tawālā).

ex.u: سِیاسَتْدان اِس واقِعَہ سے مُتَوَجَّہ ہوں گے
ex.r: siyāsatdān is vāqi'a se mutavajja hõ ge
ex.e: Politicians will be all over this occurrence

ex.u: سَب کو اُس کے طَرَف مُتَوَجَّہ کَرْنے کے لِئے وہ اَیسی بَڑی باتیں کَر رَہی ہَے
ex.r: sab ko us ke taraf mutavajja karne ke lie vo aisī baṛī bātẽ kar rahī hai
ex.e: She is saying such impressive things in order to draw everyone's attention to herself

متوجہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian متوجه (mutawajjih), from Arabic مُتَوَجِّه (mutawajjih).

متولد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُتَوَلِّد (mutawallid), from Arabic مُتَوَلِّد (mutawallid).

senses.g: superintendent or treasurer (of a mosque, etc.); administrator, procurator, or trustee (of a religious or charitable foundation)

متی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian متی (mattā), from Arabic مَتَّى (mattā). The shift from final -ā to -ī could be due to mispronunciation of the alif maqsura.

senses.g: Mathura, Muttra (a city, the administrative center of Mathura district, Uttar Pradesh, India)
Mathura (a district of Uttar Pradesh, India)
Mathura (a Lok Sabha constituency in Uttar Pradesh, India)
fatness; plumpness (ie. the state of being fat)

مٹر.e: Inherited from Proto-New Indo-Aryan *𑀫𑀝𑁆𑀝𑀭 (*maṭṭara). Compare موٹھ (moṭh). Cognate with Bengali মটর (moṭor).

مٹی.e: Borrowed from Punjabi مِٹّی (miṭṭī) / ਮਿੱਟੀ (miṭṭī), from Sanskrit मृत्तिका (mṛttikā). Doublet of ماٹی (māṭī).

ex.u: دریا کے پانی میں مٹھاس ہے۔
ex.r: daryā ke pānī mẽ miṭhās hai.
ex.e: There is sweetness in the river water.

مٹھی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑀼𑀝𑁆𑀞𑀺 (muṭṭhi) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit मुष्टि (muṣṭi), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *muštíš (“fist”). Doublet of مُشْت (muśt). Cognate with Punjabi ਮੁੱਠ (muṭṭha), Bengali মুঠা (muṭha) and Persian مشت (mošt).

مثال.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مِثَال (misāl), borrowed from Arabic مِثَال (miṯāl).

senses.g: for example, for instance, e.g.; namely

مثلا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَثَلاً (masalan), from Arabic مَثَلًا (maṯalan).

مجبور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مجبور (majbūr), from Arabic مَجْبُور (majbūr).

senses.g: assemblage; gathering; meeting (large crowd, or place where people have congregated, usually for the same purpose)
crowd; throng; multitude (of both people and items)

مجمع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَجْمَع (majma'), borrowed from Arabic مَجْمَع (majmaʕ), from جَمَعَ (jamaʕa). First attested in c. 1657 as Middle Hindi مجمع (mjm').

مجمع الجزائر.e: Borrowed from Persian مجمعالجزایر (majma'o-l-jazâyer), a Pseudo-Arabism from مَجْمَعُ الْجَزَائِر (majmaʕu al-jazāʔir)
